transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: Good morning, Lakeside Hotel, how can I help you?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Hi, I'd like to book a room for next weekend, please.

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: Of course. Could I take your name first?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, it's Daniel Whitfield. That's W-H-I-T-F-I-E-L-D.

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: Thank you, Mr Whitfield. And what dates were you looking at?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Friday the ninth to Sunday the eleventh of May. Two nights.

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: Let me check... yes, we have availability. Would you like a single or a double room?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: A double, please. Actually, do you have any rooms with a lake view?

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: We do, though they're a little more expensive — ninety-five pounds a night instead of seventy-eight.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: That's fine, I'll take the lake view.

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: Great. So that's a double room with a lake view for two nights, coming to a hundred and ninety pounds in total. Breakfast is included.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Perfect. Oh, and is there parking available?

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: Yes, there's a car park behind the hotel — it's free for guests. Could I get a contact number, in case we need to reach you?

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: Thank you. Your booking reference is LH-2214. You'll receive a confirmation email shortly.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Great, thank you very much.

transcript.speaker: Receptionist
transcript.text: You're welcome. We look forward to seeing you on the ninth.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the form below. Write ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER for each answer.

transcript.speaker: Guide
transcript.text: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to the City History Museum. Before we begin the tour, let me give you a quick overview of the building and what's on offer today. We're currently standing in the main entrance hall, which was actually the original ballroom when this building served as a private residence in the 1890s. The ceiling you can see above you is the original plasterwork, though it was carefully restored about ten years ago after water damage. If you look to your left, you'll see the museum shop, which sells postcards, books, and replicas of some of our most popular exhibits. It's open the same hours as the museum itself. To your right is the café, which serves light lunches and snacks until four in the afternoon — a little later than the shop, which closes at three thirty. Now, in terms of the exhibits themselves, the ground floor is dedicated to the city's early history, from its founding right through to the industrial period. The first floor houses our textiles and fashion collection, which is particularly popular with school groups. On the second floor, you'll find our newest exhibition, on maritime trade, which only opened last month. Unfortunately, the top floor, which usually contains our art collection, is closed for renovation until the autumn. If you need any assistance during your visit, staff wearing blue jackets are stationed on every floor. Photography is permitted throughout the museum, but please don't use flash, as it can damage some of the older textiles and documents. Right, let's begin our tour in the early history gallery.

questionGroups.instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

questions.statement: What was the museum building originally used for?
What time does the museum café close?
Which floor houses the maritime trade exhibition?
Why is the top floor currently closed?

questions.statement: What colour jackets do museum staff wear?

questionGroups.instructions: Which floor is each exhibit on? Choose the correct floor for questions 16–20.

questions.statement: Early city history exhibit
Textiles and fashion collection
Maritime trade exhibition
Art collection
Museum shop

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: Come in, Emma. So, how's the research project coming along?

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: Good, thanks. I've been focusing on tidal energy, like we discussed, but I've run into a few problems with my literature review.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: What kind of problems?

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: Well, most of the studies I've found are quite technical — they focus on engineering aspects, but I want to look more at the economic viability of tidal projects, and there's much less written about that.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: That's actually a common issue in this field. Have you looked at the Severn Estuary case study? There was a proposal for a large tidal barrage there that was eventually rejected, partly on cost grounds.

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: I have looked at it briefly, but I hadn't thought about using it as a central case study.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: I'd recommend building your whole second chapter around it. It gives you a concrete example to analyse rather than just summarising the general literature.

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: That's a good idea. Should I also compare it with a project that did go ahead, for contrast?

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: Yes, that would strengthen your argument considerably. The MeyGen project in Scotland would work well — it's one of the few tidal stream projects that's actually operating commercially.

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: Okay, I'll look into that. I'm also a bit worried about my timeline. I still need to interview two industry experts, and neither has confirmed yet.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: How many words have you written so far?

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: About three thousand, out of the required eight thousand.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: That's not bad for this stage. I'd suggest sending a follow-up email to your interview contacts this week — don't leave it until the deadline is close.

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: I will. One more thing — for the methodology section, do you think I should include a survey as well as the interviews?

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: I don't think that's necessary, given your word limit. Two well-chosen interviews will give you more depth than a survey would. I'd rather see you spend that time strengthening your data analysis.

transcript.speaker: Emma
transcript.text: Okay, that makes sense. Thank you, that's really helpful.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: No problem. Let's meet again in two weeks — bring a draft of the Severn Estuary chapter if you can.

questionGroups.instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

questions.statement: What is Emma's research project mainly about?
What problem does Emma say she has with the existing literature?
options: It's mostly about engineering, not economics

questions.statement: Why does the tutor suggest the Severn Estuary case study?
options: It was a very successful project
It gives a concrete example to analyse
Emma has already researched it in depth
It is the largest tidal project in the world

questions.statement: What does the tutor say about the MeyGen project?
options: It failed due to high costs
It is still only a proposal

questions.statement: What does the tutor advise Emma NOT to include in her methodology?

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Write NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS for each answer.

questions.prompt: How many industry experts does Emma still need to interview?
How many words has Emma written so far?
What is Emma's total required word count?
What does the tutor tell Emma to do this week regarding her interview contacts?
What should Emma bring to the next meeting?
answer: draft of the severn estuary chapter

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: Good morning, everyone. Today I want to talk about the global history of coffee — a plant that began as a regional curiosity and became one of the most widely traded commodities in the world. The coffee plant is native to the highlands of Ethiopia, where, according to popular legend, a goat herder named Kaldi first noticed his goats becoming unusually energetic after eating berries from a certain shrub. While this story is almost certainly more folklore than fact, historical records do confirm that coffee was being cultivated in Ethiopia by at least the ninth century. From there, coffee cultivation and consumption spread to the Arabian Peninsula, particularly Yemen, by the fifteenth century, where it became closely associated with Sufi religious practice, used to help monks stay awake during long nights of prayer. Coffee houses, known as qahveh khaneh, began appearing in cities across the Middle East during the sixteenth century, functioning as important centres of social and intellectual life, where people gathered to talk, listen to music, and play games, as well as to drink coffee. These coffee houses became so influential that they were sometimes referred to as 'schools of the wise,' since so much political and cultural discussion took place within them. European travellers encountered coffee in the Middle East and brought it back to the continent in the seventeenth century, where it initially met with some suspicion — in Italy, some clergy even argued the drink should be banned as an invention of the devil, before Pope Clement the Eighth reportedly tried it himself and gave it his approval. From Europe, coffee cultivation spread to the colonies, with the Dutch establishing plantations in Java, in modern-day Indonesia, and the French introducing the plant to the Caribbean. It was a single coffee plant, given as a gift to King Louis the Fourteenth, that is said to have been the ancestor of nearly all coffee plants later grown throughout Latin America, following a rather dramatic sea voyage in which a young naval officer supposedly protected the seedling with his own share of drinking water. By the eighteenth century, coffee had become a major global commodity, and today it remains one of the most valuable agricultural exports in the world, second only to crude oil among globally traded commodities according to some estimates, supporting the livelihoods of an estimated hundred and twenty-five million people worldwide.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the notes below. Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.

questions.prompt: Coffee is believed to have originated in the highlands of ___.
According to legend, ___ first noticed the effects of coffee berries on his goats.
In Yemen, coffee became closely associated with ___ religious practice.
Middle Eastern coffee houses were sometimes called "schools of the ___."
The Dutch established coffee plantations in ___, in modern-day Indonesia.

questionGroups.instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

questions.statement: Who is said to have approved of coffee after initially facing opposition from clergy?
How did a single coffee plant reportedly spread to Latin America?
options: It was smuggled by Dutch traders
It was given as a gift and taken by ship
It grew wild after being carried by birds
It was purchased by Ethiopian farmers

questions.statement: What did the naval officer reportedly sacrifice to protect the coffee seedling?

tests.title: A Guide to the Cultural Quarter

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: Good afternoon, and welcome to this recorded guide to the city's Cultural Quarter. I'll take you through five of the area's best-known venues, so you can plan your visit. First, the Old Mill Gallery. This is a contemporary art space housed in a converted nineteenth-century flour mill, right on the riverbank. It's particularly known for hosting emerging local artists, and entry is completely free, though donations are welcomed. It's open every day except Monday. Next is the Riverside Theatre, our largest performance venue, seating just over eight hundred people. It hosts a mixture of touring productions and local drama groups, and it's especially popular for its annual Shakespeare festival each summer. The Museum of Craft focuses on traditional and contemporary craftsmanship — woodwork, textiles, ceramics, and glassblowing. Uniquely among the venues on this tour, it offers hands-on workshops most weekends, where visitors can try a craft themselves under the guidance of a working artisan. The Central Library, meanwhile, isn't just for borrowing books. Its top floor houses a small archive of historical photographs of the city, which is open to the public free of charge on weekday afternoons. And finally, the Botanical Conservatory, a large glasshouse containing over two thousand plant species from around the world. It's especially popular with families, and it's the only one of the five venues that charges a standard admission fee, currently six pounds fifty for adults.

transcript.speaker: Narrator
transcript.text: Let me give you a little more detail. The Old Mill Gallery is open from ten in the morning until six in the evening, and there's a small café inside called The Kiln, which serves light lunches. The Riverside Theatre box office is located on Mill Street, just a two-minute walk from the gallery, and the nearest bus stop is Riverside Quay, served by the number twelve bus. For group visits of ten or more to the Botanical Conservatory, there is a twenty percent discount on the admission price, but you'll need to book at least forty-eight hours in advance.

Hosts a festival dedicated to a single playwright each year.
Allows visitors to practise a craft themselves.

Charges a standard admission fee.

transcript.speaker: Tutor

transcript.speaker: Mateo

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: Okay. One thing I'd suggest: make sure your case studies come from at least two different continents, not just Europe.

transcript.speaker: Priya

transcript.speaker: Tutor

transcript.speaker: Mateo

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: And do you need any equipment beyond the usual projector?

transcript.speaker: Tutor

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: Today I want to look at motivation — specifically, the distinction psychologists draw between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation, and why that distinction matters so much in education and the workplace. Intrinsic motivation refers to engaging in an activity for its own sake, because it's inherently interesting or satisfying. Extrinsic motivation, by contrast, involves doing something to obtain an external reward or avoid a punishment — a salary, a grade, praise from a parent. For decades, it was widely assumed that any motivation was better than none. But a series of experiments beginning in the early nineteen seventies challenged this. Psychologists Edward Deci and Richard Ryan found that offering people external rewards for tasks they already found intrinsically enjoyable often reduced their subsequent interest in that task once the reward was removed — a phenomenon now known as the overjustification effect.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: This led Deci and Ryan to develop what's called self-determination theory, which proposes that human motivation depends on satisfying three basic psychological needs: autonomy, meaning a sense of control over one's own actions; competence, meaning a sense of being effective and capable; and relatedness, meaning a sense of connection to others. This theory has had significant influence in education. Classrooms that offer students meaningful choices, appropriately challenging tasks, and supportive relationships with teachers tend to produce more durable, self-sustaining motivation than classrooms relying heavily on grades and external pressure alone.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: It's worth noting, though, that self-determination theory doesn't claim external rewards are always harmful. Researchers now generally distinguish between controlling rewards, which are experienced as pressure, and informational rewards, which simply provide feedback on competence without feeling coercive. A genuine, specific compliment on a piece of work, for instance, tends not to undermine intrinsic motivation the way a cash bonus for the same task often does. One further complication is cultural variation. Some cross-cultural studies suggest the importance of autonomy, in particular, may be somewhat greater in individualist societies than in more collectivist ones, where relatedness may play a comparatively larger motivational role, though this remains an active area of ongoing research, and the findings are not yet entirely settled.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the summary below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the box for each answer.
questionGroups.summaryText: Deci and Ryan's research found that external rewards could sometimes reduce interest in a task that was already enjoyable, a pattern they called the ___31___ effect. This led to self-determination theory, which holds that motivation depends on three needs: ___32___, a sense of control; ___33___, a sense of being capable; and ___34___, a sense of connection to others. Later researchers distinguished controlling rewards from ___35___ rewards, which give feedback without feeling coercive.

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS for each answer.

questions.prompt: In what decade did Deci and Ryan's early experiments begin?
What term describes rewards that are experienced as pressure?
Give an example of an informational reward mentioned in the lecture.
In which type of society might relatedness play a comparatively larger motivational role?
How does the lecturer describe the current state of research into cultural variation in motivation?

tests.title: Orientation Talk for New Volunteers at a Nature Reserve

transcript.speaker: Coordinator
transcript.text: Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the volunteer induction here at Foxglove Nature Reserve. I'll briefly run through the five main volunteering roles we offer, so you can decide which suits you best. First, Trail Maintenance. Volunteers in this role help clear overgrown paths, repair fencing, and replace damaged signage. It's physically active work, usually outdoors in small teams, and no previous experience is required — full training and equipment are provided on your first session. Second, Visitor Centre Reception. This role is based indoors, welcoming visitors, answering questions, and handling small retail sales in our gift shop. It suits volunteers who enjoy talking to the public, and a friendly manner matters more than specific qualifications.

transcript.speaker: Coordinator
transcript.text: Third, Wildlife Monitoring. This involves recording sightings of birds, mammals, and insects during scheduled surveys, and the data collected feeds directly into national conservation databases. Some background knowledge of local species is genuinely useful here, though we do run a short training course for anyone keen to learn. Fourth, Habitat Restoration. Volunteers assist with tasks like removing invasive plant species, planting native trees, and restoring wetland areas. It's seasonal work, most active between autumn and early spring, and can involve fairly strenuous physical activity. And finally, Educational Tours. Volunteers in this role lead guided walks for school groups and members of the public, sharing knowledge about the reserve's ecology and history. This is the only one of the five roles that requires you to complete a short public-speaking assessment before you begin.

transcript.speaker: Coordinator
transcript.text: Let me add a few more practical details. Trail Maintenance sessions run every Saturday, starting at nine in the morning. The Visitor Centre needs cover mainly on weekday afternoons, from one until five. Wildlife Monitoring surveys typically last around two hours, and volunteers are asked to commit to at least one survey per month. For Habitat Restoration, please wear sturdy boots, as some sites can be quite muddy. And anyone interested in Educational Tours should contact the reserve's learning officer directly, whose name is Fiona Marsh.

questionGroups.instructions: Match each description below with the correct volunteering role, A–E.

Involves recording data that feeds into national conservation databases.
Is most active between autumn and early spring.

Suits volunteers who enjoy talking to the public in an indoor setting.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: Come in. So, how's the literature review coming along?

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: It's mostly done — I've got about forty sources now, though I think I need a few more on the methodology side.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: That's a reasonable number for this stage. What's your research question again, remind me?

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: I'm looking at whether remote working affects employee wellbeing differently depending on job seniority.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: Good, that's nicely specific. What method are you planning to use?

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: I'm planning a mixed-methods approach — an online survey, plus follow-up interviews with about ten participants.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: How many responses are you hoping to get for the survey?

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: I'm aiming for at least one hundred and fifty, ideally more.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: That should give you enough for meaningful statistical analysis. When do you plan to launch the survey?

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: I was hoping to send it out in the second week of February.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: That's fine, just make sure ethical approval comes through first — have you submitted the application?

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: Yes, I submitted it last Monday, so I'm expecting a response within the next two weeks.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: Good. And your final submission deadline is still the fifteenth of May?

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: That's right.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: Okay. One suggestion — for the interviews, I'd recommend recruiting participants from at least two different industries, not just one, to strengthen your findings.

transcript.speaker: Hana
transcript.text: That makes sense, I'll broaden my recruitment.

transcript.speaker: Supervisor
transcript.text: Good. Let's meet again in three weeks to review your survey draft.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: Today's lecture looks at the Human Genome Project, one of the most significant scientific undertakings of the twentieth century. Launched in 1990 as an international collaborative effort, the project set out to determine the complete sequence of DNA base pairs that make up the human genome, and to identify and map all of the genes it contains. It was originally expected to take fifteen years to complete.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: In fact, thanks to rapid technological improvements in sequencing methods during the project's later years, a working draft of the genome was announced in the year 2000, and the project was declared essentially complete in 2003, two years ahead of the original schedule. The final cost came to approximately three billion dollars, funded primarily by the United States government alongside international partners in the United Kingdom, Japan, Germany, France, and China.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: One of the most surprising early findings was the number of genes involved. Before the project began, many scientists estimated the human genome would contain somewhere between eighty thousand and a hundred thousand genes. The actual figure, once sequencing was complete, turned out to be far lower — approximately twenty thousand — a discovery that overturned prior assumptions about the relationship between the number of genes an organism has and the complexity of that organism.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: The project has had enormously wide-ranging practical applications. In medicine, it has enabled more precise diagnosis of genetic disorders and underpins the growing field of personalised medicine, in which treatments can, in principle, be tailored to an individual's specific genetic profile. It has also contributed significantly to forensic science, agricultural research, and our understanding of human evolutionary history.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: It's worth noting that the project's findings raised significant ethical questions from the outset, particularly around genetic privacy and the potential for discrimination based on genetic information — for instance, by employers or insurance companies. In response, around five percent of the project's total budget was deliberately allocated to research specifically examining the ethical, legal, and social implications of the research, a dedicated funding stream that was, at the time, considered a genuinely novel approach for a scientific project of this scale.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the summary below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the box for each answer.
questionGroups.summaryText: A working ___31___ of the human genome was announced in 2000, and the project finished two years ___32___ of schedule. Sequencing revealed approximately twenty thousand ___33___, far fewer than originally estimated. The project's findings have supported the growth of ___34___ medicine, though they also raised significant ___35___ questions around privacy and discrimination.

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS for each answer.

questions.prompt: In what year was the Human Genome Project launched?
What was the approximate final cost of the project?
What was the higher end of the early estimate for the number of genes?
What percentage of the total budget was allocated to ethical, legal, and social research?
Besides medicine, name one other field the project has contributed to.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Good afternoon, Swift Removals, how can I help?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Hi, I'm moving flat next month and I'd like a quote, please.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Of course. Could I take your name?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, it's Grace Okafor.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Thanks, Grace. What's the address you're moving from?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Twenty-two Bellview Road.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: And where are you moving to?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Forty-five Kingfisher Close, on the other side of town.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Right. And roughly how many rooms are we talking about?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: It's a two-bedroom flat.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Okay. Do you need us to supply packing boxes, or will you handle the packing yourself?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: I'd like you to supply boxes, if that's included.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: It is, up to twenty boxes free of charge — after that it's fifty pence per box. Now, what date were you thinking?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: The fourteenth of April, if possible — it's a Saturday.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Let me check... yes, we have a slot. For a two-bedroom flat with boxes supplied, the total would come to three hundred and eighty pounds.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: That sounds reasonable.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Great. I'll need a twenty percent deposit to confirm the booking — that's seventy-six pounds.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: That's fine, I can pay that today.

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Perfect. And could I take a contact number, in case the team needs to reach you on the day?

transcript.speaker: Agent
transcript.text: Thank you. I'll email your confirmation and invoice.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the form below. Write ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER for each answer.

transcript.speaker: Guide
transcript.text: Good morning, and welcome to this walking tour of the old town. Over the next hour, we'll visit five historic sites, so let me give you a quick overview before we set off. Our first stop, the Old Town Hall, dates back to the fifteenth century and served as the seat of local government for over five hundred years; today it houses a small museum documenting the city's medieval trade guilds. From there, we'll walk to the Cathedral, whose tallest spire remained, for almost two centuries, the tallest structure in the entire country.

transcript.speaker: Guide
transcript.text: Next, we'll cross Merchant's Bridge, the oldest surviving bridge in the city, originally built to allow traders to cross the river without paying the ferry toll charged at the time. We'll then pass the Clock Tower, famous for its mechanical figures that emerge and perform a short sequence on the hour, a tradition that has continued uninterrupted since the tower was built. And finally, we'll finish at the Riverside Market, which has operated on the same site continuously since the twelfth century, making it one of the oldest working markets in Europe.

transcript.speaker: Guide
transcript.text: A few practical notes. The Old Town Hall museum is free to enter for tour groups, though individual visitors pay a small admission fee. The Cathedral's spire can be climbed for an additional fee of four pounds, though be warned, it's over three hundred steps. The Clock Tower's mechanical figures perform every hour on the hour, so if we time it right, we should catch the one o'clock performance. The Riverside Market is busiest on Saturdays, so if you're planning to return, a weekday visit tends to be considerably quieter. Please note that photography is not permitted inside the Cathedral, though it's welcomed everywhere else on the tour.

questionGroups.instructions: Match each description below with the correct site, A–E.

questions.statement: Was originally built to help traders avoid paying a toll.
Has operated continuously on the same site since the twelfth century.
Was once the tallest structure in the country.
Now houses a museum about medieval trade guilds.
Features mechanical figures that perform on the hour.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the sentences below. Write ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER for each answer.

questions.prompt: Individual visitors, unlike tour groups, must pay an admission ___ for the Old Town Hall museum.
Climbing the Cathedral spire costs an additional £___.
The Riverside Market is busiest on ___.
Photography is not permitted inside the ___.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: So, I've read through your draft on the causes of the Industrial Revolution. Overall, it's a solid piece of work.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: Thanks, I was worried about the structure especially.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: The structure's actually one of its strengths. My main concern is your second section, on coal and steam power — it's currently about four hundred words, but the rest of your sections average around six hundred, so it feels underdeveloped by comparison.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: I did struggle to find enough sources for that part.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: Try looking at the Journal of Economic History — there's a good article there by Kanefsky and Robey specifically on early steam engine adoption rates.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: I'll look that up, thank you.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: Your referencing is generally accurate, though I noticed three citations in your bibliography that don't actually appear anywhere in the main text — make sure every source you list is actually cited somewhere.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: Okay, I'll go through and check that.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: Your introduction is good, but the thesis statement itself could be sharper — at the moment it reads as quite descriptive, rather than making a clear argument.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: Right, I'll try to make my actual position clearer.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: Also, your word count currently stands at two thousand two hundred, but the assignment limit is two thousand five hundred, so you do have some room to expand that coal and steam section.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: Good, that helps.

transcript.speaker: Tutor
transcript.text: One last thing — the deadline for the final submission is the ninth of December, but I'd recommend sending me a revised draft by the second, so I have time to give you one more round of feedback before then.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: I'll aim for the second, then.

rows: Section on coal and steam power

questionGroups.instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

questions.statement: What is the tutor's main concern about the essay?
options: The underdeveloped coal and steam power section

questions.statement: What issue does the tutor identify with the referencing?
options: No sources are cited at all
Some bibliography entries are never cited in the text
All citations use the wrong format

questions.statement: What does the tutor say about the thesis statement?
options: It could make a clearer argument

questions.statement: How much room does Tom have to expand his essay?
options: None, he is already at the limit

tests.title: Urban Migration and the Growth of Megacities

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: Today's lecture examines one of the most significant demographic shifts of the modern era: the mass migration of people from rural areas into cities, and the resulting growth of so-called megacities — urban areas with a population exceeding ten million people. In 1950, only two cities, New York and Tokyo, qualified as megacities by this definition. Today, there are more than thirty, and the United Nations projects the number could exceed forty by 2035.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: The primary driver of rural-to-urban migration is, overwhelmingly, economic: cities typically offer significantly higher wages and a much wider range of employment opportunities than rural areas, particularly in manufacturing and service industries. Access to better healthcare, education, and infrastructure are also consistently cited as significant secondary factors in migration surveys.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: This rapid growth, however, brings substantial challenges. Many megacities, particularly in the Global South, have struggled to expand infrastructure quickly enough to keep pace with population growth, resulting in what urban planners term informal settlements — commonly known as slums — that often lack reliable access to clean water, sanitation, or secure land tenure. The United Nations estimates that roughly one billion people worldwide currently live in such informal settlements.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: In response, some cities have adopted what is known as inclusionary urban planning, which deliberately incorporates affordable housing requirements and improved public transport access into new development projects, rather than allowing infrastructure to expand haphazardly in response to demand alone. Curitiba, in Brazil, is frequently cited as an early and particularly successful example, having redesigned its public bus network in the 1970s in a way that continues to influence transport planning internationally to this day.

transcript.speaker: Lecturer
transcript.text: Looking ahead, most demographers expect urban migration to continue for at least several more decades, particularly across sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, regions that are expected to account for the majority of global urban population growth between now and 2050. This makes the question of how cities plan for growth, rather than merely reacting to it after the fact, one of the central urban policy challenges of the twenty-first century.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the summary below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the box for each answer.
questionGroups.summaryText: The primary driver of rural-to-urban migration is overwhelmingly ___31___, though access to healthcare and education are cited as ___32___ factors. Rapid growth has led to the spread of ___33___ settlements lacking basic services. Some cities have responded with ___34___ urban planning, which builds affordable housing and transport access into new projects. The central challenge for cities is planning for growth rather than simply ___35___ to it.

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS for each answer.

questions.prompt: How many megacities existed in 1950, according to the lecture?
What number of megacities does the UN project could exist by 2035?
Roughly how many people currently live in informal settlements, according to the UN?
Which Brazilian city is cited as an early example of inclusionary planning?
In what decade did Curitiba redesign its public bus network?
